topic: AnythingLLM Review 2026: Local AI Knowledge Base and Agent Runtime

competitors.title: AnythingLLM Review 2026: Best Free Self-Hosted AI Assistant
key_points: Best free chat-with-docs application tested in 2026
MCP support pushes it from 'nice to have' to genuinely useful
Desktop app for single users, Docker deployment for multi-user teams
Supports Ollama and LM Studio as local inference backends
Bundles LanceDB for vector storage out of the box

competitors.title: AnythingLLM Review (2025): Local AI, RAG, Agents & Setup Guide
key_points: Supports 30+ LLM providers including OpenAI, Anthropic, Ollama, Groq, Mistral, DeepSeek
Workspace concept organizes documents and conversations into isolated knowledge bases
LanceDB is the default local vector store — data never leaves the instance
Embedding models are set system-wide and cannot be changed per-workspace without re-embedding
Agent mode enables tool use: web search, file operations, code execution

competitors.title: AnythingLLM Review 2026: Features, Pricing, Pros & Cons, Alternatives
key_points: MIT licensed and completely free for self-hosting
Multi-user Docker deployment supports Admin, Manager, and Default roles
White-labeling and custom branding supported for enterprise deployments
Embeddable chat widgets for websites and internal portals
No API key required for local-only usage with Ollama

competitors.title: Is AnythingLLM the All-in-One AI App You Need? A Deep Review
key_points: Targets technical users comfortable with Docker and CLI
UX described as 'clunky' by some users; RAG quality requires tuning
Needs 16GB RAM minimum; GPU with 8GB+ VRAM for acceptable speed
Self-hosting ops overhead is a real cost vs. managed SaaS
Not a plug-and-play solution for non-technical teams

competitors.title: AnythingLLM: Complete Guide to Setup, RAG, and Use Cases
key_points: Enterprise-grade solution for custom chatbots using RAG pattern
OpenAI-compatible API allows integration with existing tooling
Three deployment modes: desktop app, Docker self-hosted, managed cloud
Multi-user authentication via Simple SSO Passthrough with API-provisioned users
Workspace chat bubble embeddable on documentation sites and internal portals

stats.fact: AnythingLLM has 54,000–57,000+ GitHub stars on mintplex-labs/anything-llm as of early 2026
Supports 30+ LLM providers including OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Ollama, LM Studio, Azure, AWS Bedrock, Groq, Together, Mistral, and DeepSeek
Supports 9+ vector database options; LanceDB is the default on-instance store
Desktop app minimum requirement: 2GB RAM; Docker deployment recommends 4GB RAM
A 7B parameter model generates responses in 30–60 seconds on CPU versus 2–3 seconds on a GPU
Accuracy Optimized mode (reranking) adds 100–500ms to response time but yields better retrieval results
MCP (Model Context Protocol) support was added in AnythingLLM Desktop v1.8.0
MIT licensed — completely free for self-hosting with no per-seat cost
Agent Flows provides a visual canvas for chaining API calls, LLM instructions, and file operations without code
AnythingLLM is not an inference engine — it delegates model execution to Ollama, LM Studio, or cloud providers

angles: Privacy-first AI stack: 100% local operation with no data leaving your system — ideal for healthcare, legal, and finance teams with compliance requirements
The 'all-in-one' advantage: RAG + multi-agent runtime + multi-user workspaces in a single tool vs. assembling a LangChain/LlamaIndex stack from scratch
MCP integration in v1.8.0 turns AnythingLLM into a live knowledge backend for Claude Desktop and other MCP-aware clients
AnythingLLM as the orchestration layer bridging local inference (Ollama/LM Studio) and production-ready enterprise RAG
Workspace isolation as a productivity strategy: separate knowledge bases per project, team, or client without namespace collisions
The hidden hardware cost: GPU with 8GB+ VRAM needed for real-time performance — CPU inference is technically free but practically unusable at scale
Visual Agent Flows vs. code-first frameworks: who benefits and where the ceiling is
AnythingLLM vs. Open WebUI architecture debate: RAG-first vs. chat-first design philosophies
